│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF3 | │ | Addons | @chromatic-com/storybook, @storybook/addon-vitest, │ @storybook/addon-a11y, @storybook/addon-docs, │ @storybook/addon-onboarding | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorat...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?rende...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/pla...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/v...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report

Based on my thorough analysis of the codebase at `/Users/kasperpeulen/.cursor/worktrees/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-22T14-06-09Z-e5e8aa33/project`, here is a complete understanding of the full runtime environment:

│   ├── landing/

    ├── button.css
    ├── header.css
    ├── page.css
    └── assets/
```

---

### 3. ENTRY POINTS

**src/index.jsx:**
```jsx
import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client";
import App from "./App";
import "./core-ui/index.css";

const container = document.getElementById("root");
const root = createRoot(container);
root.render(<App />);
```

**index.html:**
- Root element: `<main id="root" class="m-0 p-0"></main>`
- No portal root divs defined in HTML
- Loads Google reCAPTCHA v2 script globally
- Links core-ui/index.css

---

### 4. ROOT APP COMPONENT (src/App.jsx)

**Key Features:**
- **Router:** BrowserRouter (react-router-dom v7.7.0)
- **State Management:** React hooks only (no Context/Provider pattern)
  - `user` (useState)
  - `loggedIn` (useState)
  - `triggeredLogout` (useState)
- **Data Source:** Airtable API (via custom edgyBase)
- **Authentication:** Email/password validation against Airtable database

**Main Functions:**
- `retrieveDatabase(email, password)` - Fetches users from Airtable
- `registerUser(data)` - Creates new user in Airtable
- `userEmailExists(email)` - Checks email uniqueness
- `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - Updates user in Airtable
- `deleteUser(userId)` - Deletes user from Airtable

**Global Routes:**
```
/                   → Landing (shows loggedIn state)
/sign-in            → SignIn OR Profile (if logged in)
/sign-up            → SignUp OR Profile (if logged in)
/profile            → Profile (protected, shows NotFound if not logged in)
/pricing            → Pricing (shows loggedIn state)
/contact            → Contact
/faq                → Faq
/blog               → Blog
/blog/:name         → BlogPost
/about              → About
/terms              → Terms
/refunds            → Refunds
/privacy            → Privacy
/jobs               → Jobs
/*                  → NotFound
```

---

### 5. PROVIDERS & CONTEXTS

**NO PROVIDERS USED.** The application uses only:
- React hooks (useState, useEffect)
- React Router (BrowserRouter, Routes, Route, Link, useNavigate)
- Direct prop passing

No Context API, Redux, Zustand, or other state management libraries.

---

### 6. CSS & STYLING

**CSS Framework:** Tailwind CSS v4.1.11 (via @tailwindcss/vite)

**Global CSS Files:**
1. **src/core-ui/index.css** - Main global stylesheet
   - Tailwind import
   - CSS variables: `--primary-font-color` (rgb(147, 157, 250)), `--primary-font-color-hover` (rgb(82, 98, 245))
   - System font stack setup
   - Code block styling

2. **Storybook CSS files:**
   - src/stories/button.css
   - src/stories/header.css
   - src/stories/page.css

**Styling Approach:** Mostly Tailwind utility classes + custom CSS variables for brand colors

---

### 7. DATA FETCHING & BROWSER STATE HOOKS

**Browser Storage Used:**

1. **sessionStorage (Primary):**
   - `loggedIn` - Boolean flag indicating login status
   - `user` - JSON stringified user object containing: id, email, fullname, gender, plan, password, createdTime

2. **localStorage:** NOT USED

3. **Cookies:** NOT USED

4. **Custom Hooks:** NONE - All state management is direct useState/useEffect

**Data Fetching Patterns:**

1. **Airtable API Calls:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for database reads/writes
   - Direct fetch to Airtable via custom edgyBase instance
   - Methods: `edgyBase("users").create()`, `.destroy()`

2. **reCAPTCHA Verification:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA validation
   - `react-google-recaptcha` package (v3.1.0)
   - Verified via POST to server with secret key

3. **Environment Variables:**
   ```
   VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY          - Airtable API key
   VITE_AIRTABLE_ID           - Airtable base ID
   VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL   - Server URL for Airtable proxy
   VITE_CAPTCHA_KEY           - reCAPTCHA site key
   VITE_CAPTCHA_SECRET        - reCAPTCHA secret (exposed in client - SECURITY ISSUE)
   VITE_SERVER_URL            - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA verification
   ```

---

### 8. PORTAL ROOTS

**No portal roots defined.** The application does not use `ReactDOM.createPortal()`.

However, the following modals are rendered directly in the component tree:

1. **Profile.jsx** - Delete account confirmation modal (fixed position overlay)
2. **Profile.jsx** - Edit password modal (fixed position overlay)

These use `fixed inset-0 z-10` with background overlay but are NOT portals.

---

### 9. PAGE COMPONENTS

**Main Page Routes:**
1. **Landing** - `/` - Hero, features, pricing, testimonials, team, comparison
2. **Pricing** - `/pricing` - Pricing table, comparison, FAQ
3. **Blog** - `/blog` - Blog grid listing
4. **BlogPost** - `/blog/:name` - Individual blog post
5. **About** - `/about` - Team info, company history
6. **Contact** - `/contact` - Contact form
7. **FAQ** - `/faq` - FAQ list
8. **Jobs** - `/jobs` - Job listings
9. **SignIn** - `/sign-in` - Sign in form
10. **SignUp** - `/sign-up` - Sign up form with reCAPTCHA
11. **Profile** - `/profile` - User profile editor (protected)
12. **Terms** - `/terms` - Terms of service
13. **Privacy** - `/privacy` - Privacy policy
14. **Refunds** - `/refunds` - Refund policy
15. **NotFound** - `/*` - 404 page

---

### 10. SHARED/REUSABLE COMPONENTS

**Layout Components:**
- **Navigation** - Top navigation bar with conditional auth buttons
- **Footer** - Footer with links, contact info, social links

**Feature Components:**
- **Cta** - "Call to Action" section (conditioned on loggedIn state)
- **Team** - Team member grid display
- **Newsletter** - Email subscription form with validation

**Utility Components:**
- **NotFound** - 404 page with navigation suggestions

**Button Component (Storybook):**
- Generic button with size and style variants (primary/secondary)

---

### 11. PACKAGE.JSON

**Key Dependencies:**
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "^18.3.1",
    "react-dom": "^18.3.1",
    "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0",
    "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0",
    "airtable": "^0.12.2",
    "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0",
    "uuid": "^11.1.0",
    "moment": "^2.30.1",
    "cors": "^2.8.5",
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "dotenv": "^17.2.0",
    "node-fetch": "^3.3.2"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0",
    "vite": "^7.0.5",
    "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11",
    "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "vitest": "^4.1.2",
    "playwright": "^1.58.2",
    "@vitest/browser-playwright": "^4.1.2"
  }
}
```

**Key Scripts:**
```bash
npm run dev              # Start Vite dev server
npm run build           # Build for production
npm run preview         # Preview production build
npm run server          # Node backend (api/index.mjs)
npm run storybook       # Start Storybook on port 6006
npm run build-storybook # Build Storybook
npm run vitest:storybook # Run Storybook tests
```

---

### 12. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

**.storybook/main.ts:**
```typescript
const config: StorybookConfig = {
  stories: [
    '../src/**/*.mdx',
    '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
    './eval-support/*.mdx',
  ],
  addons: [
    '@chromatic-com/storybook',
    '@storybook/addon-vitest',
    '@storybook/addon-a11y',
    '@storybook/addon-docs',
    '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
  ],
  framework: '@storybook/react-vite',
};
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx:**
```typescript
const preview: Preview = {
  parameters: {
    controls: {
      matchers: {
        color: /(background|color)$/i,
        date: /Date$/i,
      },
    },
    a11y: {
      test: 'todo',
    },
  },
};
```

**Story Files:**
- src/stories/Button.stories.js
- src/stories/Header.stories.js
- src/stories/Page.stories.js

---

### 13. BUILD CONFIGURATION

**vite.config.js:**
- React plugin (@vitejs/plugin-react)
- Tailwind CSS plugin
- Vitest configuration with Storybook plugin
- Playwright browser testing setup
- History API fallback for SPA routing
- CSS code splitting enabled

**tailwind.config.js:**
- Content glob: `["./index.html", "./src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"]`
- No custom theme extensions (uses Tailwind defaults)

---

### 14. SPECIAL FEATURES & PATTERNS

**Authentication Flow:**
1. User enters email/password on SignInForm
2. reCAPTCHA token is generated
3. Token verified via server endpoint
4. Database lookup via `retrieveDatabase(email, password)`
5. User data stored in sessionStorage
6. loggedIn state updated
7. Redirected to /profile

**User Data Storage:**
- User object in sessionStorage: `{ id, email, fullname, gender, plan, createdTime, password }`
- Password stored as plaintext in database (SECURITY ISSUE)

**Newsletter/CTA Forms:**
- Local validation only (no backend submission)
- Shows success message but doesn't persist data

**Dynamic Route Protection:**
- /profile, /sign-in, /sign-up conditionally render Profile OR public form based on loggedIn state
- No middleware/guards - all checks in route elements

---

### 15. POTENTIAL ISSUES & OBSERVATIONS

1. **No Provider Pattern:** All state is in App.jsx, props drilled throughout
2. **Password Security:** Captcha secret exposed in client code
3. **No Error Boundaries:** Unhandled errors will break the app
4. **No Suspense:** No async data loading boundaries
5. **Forms Don't Persist:** Newsletter/Hero/CTA forms validate but don't actually save
6. **No API Abstraction:** Direct fetch calls with hardcoded URLs
7. **No TypeScript:** JSX files without type safety
8. **Storybook is Preview Build:** Uses custom preview build (0.0.0-pr-34612) instead of stable version

---

### COMPLETE RUNTIME TREE SUMMARY

```
App.jsx (Root)
├── Navigation
│   ├── Links (About, Pricing, Blog, FAQ, Contact)
│   └── Auth Buttons (Sign In/Up or Sign Out/Profile)
├── Routes
│   ├── Landing
│   │   ├── Hero (with email form)
│   │   ├── Partners
│   │   ├── Feature
│   │   ├── Cta
│   │   ├── Stats
│   │   ├── Pricing (landing version)
│   │   ├── Testimonials
│   │   ├── Team
│   │   ├── Comparison
│   │   └── Newsletter
│   ├── Pricing (full page)
│   │   ├── PricingComparisonTable
│   │   ├── PricingFaq
│   │   └── Partners
│   ├── Blog/BlogPost/About/Contact/Faq/Jobs
│   ├── SignIn/SignUp (forms with reCAPTCHA)
│   ├── Profile (protected, with modals)
│   └── Legal pages (Terms/Privacy/Refunds)
└── Footer
    ├── Footer Links
    └── Social Icons
```

This is a fully functional React SPA with email authentication against Airtable, no external state management, and Tailwind CSS styling throughout.

  - `loggedIn` (useState)
  - `triggeredLogout` (useState)
- **Data Source:** Airtable API (via custom edgyBase)
- **Authentication:** Email/password validation against Airtable database

**Main Functions:**
- `retrieveDatabase(email, password)` - Fetches users from Airtable
- `registerUser(data)` - Creates new user in Airtable
- `userEmailExists(email)` - Checks email uniqueness
- `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - Updates user in Airtable
- `deleteUser(userId)` - Deletes user from Airtable

**Global Routes:**
```
/                   → Landing (shows loggedIn state)
/sign-in            → SignIn OR Profile (if logged in)
/sign-up            → SignUp OR Profile (if logged in)
/profile            → Profile (protected, shows NotFound if not logged in)
/pricing            → Pricing (shows loggedIn state)
/contact            → Contact
/faq                → Faq
/blog               → Blog
/blog/:name         → BlogPost
/about              → About
/terms              → Terms
/refunds            → Refunds
/privacy            → Privacy
/jobs               → Jobs
/*                  → NotFound
```

---

### 5. PROVIDERS & CONTEXTS

**NO PROVIDERS USED.** The application uses only:
- React hooks (useState, useEffect)
- React Router (BrowserRouter, Routes, Route, Link, useNavigate)
- Direct prop passing

No Context API, Redux, Zustand, or other state management libraries.

---

### 6. CSS & STYLING

**CSS Framework:** Tailwind CSS v4.1.11 (via @tailwindcss/vite)

**Global CSS Files:**
1. **src/core-ui/index.css** - Main global stylesheet
   - Tailwind import
   - CSS variables: `--primary-font-color` (rgb(147, 157, 250)), `--primary-font-color-hover` (rgb(82, 98, 245))
   - System font stack setup
   - Code block styling

2. **Storybook CSS files:**
   - src/stories/button.css
   - src/stories/header.css
   - src/stories/page.css

**Styling Approach:** Mostly Tailwind utility classes + custom CSS variables for brand colors

---

### 7. DATA FETCHING & BROWSER STATE HOOKS

**Browser Storage Used:**

1. **sessionStorage (Primary):**
   - `loggedIn` - Boolean flag indicating login status
   - `user` - JSON stringified user object containing: id, email, fullname, gender, plan, password, createdTime

2. **localStorage:** NOT USED

3. **Cookies:** NOT USED

4. **Custom Hooks:** NONE - All state management is direct useState/useEffect

**Data Fetching Patterns:**

1. **Airtable API Calls:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for database reads/writes
   - Direct fetch to Airtable via custom edgyBase instance
   - Methods: `edgyBase("users").create()`, `.destroy()`

2. **reCAPTCHA Verification:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA validation
   - `react-google-recaptcha` package (v3.1.0)
   - Verified via POST to server with secret key

3. **Environment Variables:**
   ```
   VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY          - Airtable API key
   VITE_AIRTABLE_ID           - Airtable base ID
   VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL   - Server URL for Airtable proxy
   VITE_CAPTCHA_KEY           - reCAPTCHA site key
   VITE_CAPTCHA_SECRET        - reCAPTCHA secret (exposed in client - SECURITY ISSUE)
   VITE_SERVER_URL            - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA verification
   ```

---

### 8. PORTAL ROOTS

**No portal roots defined.** The application does not use `ReactDOM.createPortal()`.

However, the following modals are rendered directly in the component tree:

1. **Profile.jsx** - Delete account confirmation modal (fixed position overlay)
2. **Profile.jsx** - Edit password modal (fixed position overlay)

These use `fixed inset-0 z-10` with background overlay but are NOT portals.

---

### 9. PAGE COMPONENTS

**Main Page Routes:**
1. **Landing** - `/` - Hero, features, pricing, testimonials, team, comparison
2. **Pricing** - `/pricing` - Pricing table, comparison, FAQ
3. **Blog** - `/blog` - Blog grid listing
4. **BlogPost** - `/blog/:name` - Individual blog post
5. **About** - `/about` - Team info, company history
6. **Contact** - `/contact` - Contact form
7. **FAQ** - `/faq` - FAQ list
8. **Jobs** - `/jobs` - Job listings
9. **SignIn** - `/sign-in` - Sign in form
10. **SignUp** - `/sign-up` - Sign up form with reCAPTCHA
11. **Profile** - `/profile` - User profile editor (protected)
12. **Terms** - `/terms` - Terms of service
13. **Privacy** - `/privacy` - Privacy policy
14. **Refunds** - `/refunds` - Refund policy
15. **NotFound** - `/*` - 404 page

---

### 10. SHARED/REUSABLE COMPONENTS

**Layout Components:**
- **Navigation** - Top navigation bar with conditional auth buttons
- **Footer** - Footer with links, contact info, social links

**Feature Components:**
- **Cta** - "Call to Action" section (conditioned on loggedIn state)
- **Team** - Team member grid display
- **Newsletter** - Email subscription form with validation

**Utility Components:**
- **NotFound** - 404 page with navigation suggestions

**Button Component (Storybook):**
- Generic button with size and style variants (primary/secondary)

---

### 11. PACKAGE.JSON

**Key Dependencies:**
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "^18.3.1",
    "react-dom": "^18.3.1",
    "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0",
    "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0",
    "airtable": "^0.12.2",
    "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0",
    "uuid": "^11.1.0",
    "moment": "^2.30.1",
    "cors": "^2.8.5",
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "dotenv": "^17.2.0",
    "node-fetch": "^3.3.2"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0",
    "vite": "^7.0.5",
    "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11",
    "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "vitest": "^4.1.2",
    "playwright": "^1.58.2",
    "@vitest/browser-playwright": "^4.1.2"
  }
}
```

**Key Scripts:**
```bash
npm run dev              # Start Vite dev server
npm run build           # Build for production
npm run preview         # Preview production build
npm run server          # Node backend (api/index.mjs)
npm run storybook       # Start Storybook on port 6006
npm run build-storybook # Build Storybook
npm run vitest:storybook # Run Storybook tests
```

---

### 12. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

**.storybook/main.ts:**
```typescript
const config: StorybookConfig = {
  stories: [
    '../src/**/*.mdx',
    '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
    './eval-support/*.mdx',
  ],
  addons: [
    '@chromatic-com/storybook',
    '@storybook/addon-vitest',
    '@storybook/addon-a11y',
    '@storybook/addon-docs',
    '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
  ],
  framework: '@storybook/react-vite',
};
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx:**
```typescript
const preview: Preview = {
  parameters: {
    controls: {
      matchers: {
        color: /(background|color)$/i,
        date: /Date$/i,
      },
    },
    a11y: {
      test: 'todo',
    },
  },

```

**Story Files:**
- src/stories/Button.stories.js
- src/stories/Header.stories.js
- src/stories/Page.stories.js

---

### 13. BUILD CONFIGURATION

**vite.config.js:**
- React plugin (@vitejs/plugin-react)
- Tailwind CSS plugin
- Vitest configuration with Storybook plugin
- Playwright browser testing setup
- History API fallback for SPA routing
- CSS code splitting enabled

**tailwind.config.js:**
- Content glob: `["./index.html", "./src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"]`
- No custom theme extensions (uses Tailwind defaults)

---

### 14. SPECIAL FEATURES & PATTERNS

**Authentication Flow:**
1. User enters email/password on SignInForm
2. reCAPTCHA token is generated
3. Token verified via server endpoint
4. Database lookup via `retrieveDatabase(email, password)`
5. User data stored in sessionStorage
6. loggedIn state updated
7. Redirected to /profile

**User Data Storage:**
- User object in sessionStorage: `{ id, email, fullname, gender, plan, createdTime, password }`
- Password stored as plaintext in database (SECURITY ISSUE)

**Newsletter/CTA Forms:**
- Local validation only (no backend submission)
- Shows success message but doesn't persist data

**Dynamic Route Protection:**
- /profile, /sign-in, /sign-up conditionally render Profile OR public form based on loggedIn state
- No middleware/guards - all checks in route elements

---

### 15. POTENTIAL ISSUES & OBSERVATIONS

1. **No Provider Pattern:** All state is in App.jsx, props drilled throughout
2. **Password Security:** Captcha secret exposed in client code
3. **No Error Boundaries:** Unhandled errors will break the app
4. **No Suspense:** No async data loading boundaries
5. **Forms Don't Persist:** Newsletter/Hero/CTA forms validate but don't actually save
6. **No API Abstraction:** Direct fetch calls with hardcoded URLs
7. **No TypeScript:** JSX files without type safety
8. **Storybook is Preview Build:** Uses custom preview build (0.0.0-pr-34612) instead of stable version

---

### COMPLETE RUNTIME TREE SUMMARY

```
App.jsx (Root)
├── Navigation
│   ├── Links (About, Pricing, Blog, FAQ, Contact)
│   └── Auth Buttons (Sign In/Up or Sign Out/Profile)
├── Routes
│   ├── Landing
│   │   ├── Hero (with email form)
│   │   ├── Partners
│   │   ├── Feature
│   │   ├── Cta
│   │   ├── Stats
│   │   ├── Pricing (landing version)
│   │   ├── Testimonials
│   │   ├── Team
│   │   ├── Comparison
│   │   └── Newsletter
│   ├── Pricing (full page)
│   │   ├── PricingComparisonTable
│   │   ├── PricingFaq
│   │   └── Partners
│   ├── Blog/BlogPost/About/Contact/Faq/Jobs
│   ├── SignIn/SignUp (forms with reCAPTCHA)
│   ├── Profile (protected, with modals)
│   └── Legal pages (Terms/Privacy/Refunds)
└── Footer
    ├── Footer Links
    └── Social Icons
```

This is a fully functional React SPA with email authentication against Airtable, no external state management, and Tailwind CSS styling throughout.

which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report

Based on my thorough analysis of the codebase at `/Users/kasperpeulen/.cursor/worktrees/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-22T14-06-09Z-e5e8aa33/project`, here is a complete understanding of the full runtime environment:

---

### 1. PROJECT OVERVIEW

**Project Name:** Edgy (Instagram Growth Agency)  
**Type:** React 18.3.1 + Vite + React Router v7.7.0 + Tailwind CSS  
**Package Manager:** npm (with package-lock.json)  
**Build Tool:** Vite 7.0.5  
**Testing:** Storybook 0.0.0-pr-34612 (custom preview build) with Vitest 4.1.2 + Playwright

---

### 2. FILE/DIRECTORY STRUCTURE (src/)

```
src/

---

### 3. ENTRY POINTS

**src/index.jsx:**
```jsx
import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client";
import App from "./App";
import "./core-ui/index.css";

const container = document.getElementById("root");
const root = createRoot(container);
root.render(<App />);
```

**index.html:**
- Root element: `<main id="root" class="m-0 p-0"></main>`
- No portal root divs defined in HTML
- Loads Google reCAPTCHA v2 script globally
- Links core-ui/index.css

---

### 4. ROOT APP COMPONENT (src/App.jsx)

**Key Features:**
- **Router:** BrowserRouter (react-router-dom v7.7.0)
- **State Management:** React hooks only (no Context/Provider pattern)
  - `user` (useState)
  - `loggedIn` (useState)
  - `triggeredLogout` (useState)
- **Data Source:** Airtable API (via custom edgyBase)
- **Authentication:** Email/password validation against Airtable database

**Main Functions:**
- `retrieveDatabase(email, password)` - Fetches users from Airtable
- `registerUser(data)` - Creates new user in Airtable
- `userEmailExists(email)` - Checks email uniqueness
- `updateUser(userId, formValue)` - Updates user in Airtable
- `deleteUser(userId)` - Deletes user from Airtable

**Global Routes:**
```
/                   → Landing (shows loggedIn state)
/sign-in            → SignIn OR Profile (if logged in)
/sign-up            → SignUp OR Profile (if logged in)
/profile            → Profile (protected, shows NotFound if not logged in)
/pricing            → Pricing (shows loggedIn state)
/contact            → Contact
/faq                → Faq
/blog               → Blog
/blog/:name         → BlogPost
/about              → About
/terms              → Terms
/refunds            → Refunds
/privacy            → Privacy
/jobs               → Jobs
/*                  → NotFound
```

---

### 5. PROVIDERS & CONTEXTS

**NO PROVIDERS USED.** The application uses only:
- React hooks (useState, useEffect)
- React Router (BrowserRouter, Routes, Route, Link, useNavigate)
- Direct prop passing

No Context API, Redux, Zustand, or other state management libraries.

---

### 6. CSS & STYLING

**CSS Framework:** Tailwind CSS v4.1.11 (via @tailwindcss/vite)

**Global CSS Files:**
1. **src/core-ui/index.css** - Main global stylesheet
   - Tailwind import
   - CSS variables: `--primary-font-color` (rgb(147, 157, 250)), `--primary-font-color-hover` (rgb(82, 98, 245))
   - System font stack setup
   - Code block styling

2. **Storybook CSS files:**
   - src/stories/button.css
   - src/stories/header.css
   - src/stories/page.css

**Styling Approach:** Mostly Tailwind utility classes + custom CSS variables for brand colors

---

### 7. DATA FETCHING & BROWSER STATE HOOKS

**Browser Storage Used:**

1. **sessionStorage (Primary):**
   - `loggedIn` - Boolean flag indicating login status
   - `user` - JSON stringified user object containing: id, email, fullname, gender, plan, password, createdTime

2. **localStorage:** NOT USED

3. **Cookies:** NOT USED

4. **Custom Hooks:** NONE - All state management is direct useState/useEffect

**Data Fetching Patterns:**

1. **Airtable API Calls:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for database reads/writes
   - Direct fetch to Airtable via custom edgyBase instance
   - Methods: `edgyBase("users").create()`, `.destroy()`

2. **reCAPTCHA Verification:**
   - `import.meta.env.VITE_SERVER_URL` - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA validation
   - `react-google-recaptcha` package (v3.1.0)
   - Verified via POST to server with secret key

3. **Environment Variables:**
   ```
   VITE_AIRTABLE_KEY          - Airtable API key
   VITE_AIRTABLE_ID           - Airtable base ID
   VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL   - Server URL for Airtable proxy
   VITE_CAPTCHA_KEY           - reCAPTCHA site key
   VITE_CAPTCHA_SECRET        - reCAPTCHA secret (exposed in client - SECURITY ISSUE)
   VITE_SERVER_URL            - Server endpoint for CAPTCHA verification
   ```

---

### 8. PORTAL ROOTS

**No portal roots defined.** The application does not use `ReactDOM.createPortal()`.

However, the following modals are rendered directly in the component tree:

1. **Profile.jsx** - Delete account confirmation modal (fixed position overlay)
2. **Profile.jsx** - Edit password modal (fixed position overlay)

These use `fixed inset-0 z-10` with background overlay but are NOT portals.

---

### 9. PAGE COMPONENTS

**Main Page Routes:**
1. **Landing** - `/` - Hero, features, pricing, testimonials, team, comparison
2. **Pricing** - `/pricing` - Pricing table, comparison, FAQ
3. **Blog** - `/blog` - Blog grid listing
4. **BlogPost** - `/blog/:name` - Individual blog post
5. **About** - `/about` - Team info, company history
6. **Contact** - `/contact` - Contact form
7. **FAQ** - `/faq` - FAQ list
8. **Jobs** - `/jobs` - Job listings
9. **SignIn** - `/sign-in` - Sign in form
10. **SignUp** - `/sign-up` - Sign up form with reCAPTCHA
11. **Profile** - `/profile` - User profile editor (protected)
12. **Terms** - `/terms` - Terms of service
13. **Privacy** - `/privacy` - Privacy policy
14. **Refunds** - `/refunds` - Refund policy
15. **NotFound** - `/*` - 404 page

---

### 10. SHARED/REUSABLE COMPONENTS

**Layout Components:**
- **Navigation** - Top navigation bar with conditional auth buttons
- **Footer** - Footer with links, contact info, social links

**Feature Components:**
- **Cta** - "Call to Action" section (conditioned on loggedIn state)
- **Team** - Team member grid display
- **Newsletter** - Email subscription form with validation

**Utility Components:**
- **NotFound** - 404 page with navigation suggestions

**Button Component (Storybook):**
- Generic button with size and style variants (primary/secondary)

---

### 11. PACKAGE.JSON

**Key Dependencies:**
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "^18.3.1",
    "react-dom": "^18.3.1",
    "react-router-dom": "^7.7.0",
    "@headlessui/react": "^1.5.0",
    "airtable": "^0.12.2",
    "react-google-recaptcha": "^3.1.0",
    "uuid": "^11.1.0",
    "moment": "^2.30.1",
    "cors": "^2.8.5",
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "dotenv": "^17.2.0",
    "node-fetch": "^3.3.2"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@vitejs/plugin-react": "^4.6.0",
    "vite": "^7.0.5",
    "@tailwindcss/vite": "^4.1.11",
    "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-7cb6490a",
    "vitest": "^4.1.2",
    "playwright": "^1.58.2",
    "@vitest/browser-playwright": "^4.1.2"
  }
}
```

**Key Scripts:**
```bash
npm run dev              # Start Vite dev server
npm run build           # Build for production
npm run preview         # Preview production build
npm run server          # Node backend (api/index.mjs)
npm run storybook       # Start Storybook on port 6006
npm run build-storybook # Build Storybook
npm run vitest:storybook # Run Storybook tests
```

---

### 12. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

**.storybook/main.ts:**
```typescript
const config: StorybookConfig = {
  stories: [
    '../src/**/*.mdx',
    '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',

  ],
  addons: [
    '@chromatic-com/storybook',
    '@storybook/addon-vitest',
    '@storybook/addon-a11y',

  ],
  framework: '@storybook/react-vite',

```

**.storybook/preview.tsx:**
```typescript

  parameters: {
    controls: {
      matchers: {

        date: /Date$/i,

```

**Story Files:**
- src/stories/Button.stories.js
- src/stories/Header.stories.js
- src/stories/Page.stories.js

---

### 13. BUILD CONFIGURATION

**vite.config.js:**
- React plugin (@vitejs/plugin-react)
- Tailwind CSS plugin
- Vitest configuration with Storybook plugin
- Playwright browser testing setup
- History API fallback for SPA routing
- CSS code splitting enabled

**tailwind.config.js:**
- Content glob: `["./index.html", "./src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"]`
- No custom theme extensions (uses Tailwind defaults)

---

### 14. SPECIAL FEATURES & PATTERNS

**Authentication Flow:**
1. User enters email/password on SignInForm
2. reCAPTCHA token is generated
3. Token verified via server endpoint
4. Database lookup via `retrieveDatabase(email, password)`
5. User data stored in sessionStorage
6. loggedIn state updated
7. Redirected to /profile

**User Data Storage:**
- User object in sessionStorage: `{ id, email, fullname, gender, plan, createdTime, password }`
- Password stored as plaintext in database (SECURITY ISSUE)

**Newsletter/CTA Forms:**
- Local validation only (no backend submission)
- Shows success message but doesn't persist data

**Dynamic Route Protection:**
- /profile, /sign-in, /sign-up conditionally render Profile OR public form based on loggedIn state
- No middleware/guards - all checks in route elements

---

### 15. POTENTIAL ISSUES & OBSERVATIONS

1. **No Provider Pattern:** All state is in App.jsx, props drilled throughout
2. **Password Security:** Captcha secret exposed in client code
3. **No Error Boundaries:** Unhandled errors will break the app
4. **No Suspense:** No async data loading boundaries
5. **Forms Don't Persist:** Newsletter/Hero/CTA forms validate but don't actually save
6. **No API Abstraction:** Direct fetch calls with hardcoded URLs
7. **No TypeScript:** JSX files without type safety
8. **Storybook is Preview Build:** Uses custom preview build (0.0.0-pr-34612) instead of stable version

---

### COMPLETE RUNTIME TREE SUMMARY

```
App.jsx (Root)
├── Navigation
│   ├── Links (About, Pricing, Blog, FAQ, Contact)
│   └── Auth Buttons (Sign In/Up or Sign Out/Profile)
├── Routes
│   ├── Landing
│   │   ├── Hero (with email form)
│   │   ├── Partners
│   │   ├── Feature
│   │   ├── Cta
│   │   ├── Stats
│   │   ├── Pricing (landing version)
│   │   ├── Testimonials
│   │   ├── Team
│   │   ├── Comparison
│   │   └── Newsletter
│   ├── Pricing (full page)
│   │   ├── PricingComparisonTable
│   │   ├── PricingFaq
│   │   └── Partners
│   ├── Blog/BlogPost/About/Contact/Faq/Jobs
│   ├── SignIn/SignUp (forms with reCAPTCHA)
│   ├── Profile (protected, with modals)
│   └── Legal pages (Terms/Privacy/Refunds)
└── Footer
    ├── Footer Links
    └── Social Icons
```

This is a fully functional React SPA with email authentication against Airtable, no external state management, and Tailwind CSS styling throughout.
